steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The hypothesis: shoreline hardening harms waterbird communities more than land development does. To support this, we need a comparison where hardening-heavy sites show lower IWCI than development-heavy sites — while keeping the other variable low so we can isolate each factor.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Waterbird communities at Back River, a site with a relatively high percentage of developed land and a relatively low percentage of hardened shoreline, had higher average IWCI scores than did waterbird communities at Margothy, a site with a lower percentage of developed land but a higher percentage of hardened shoreline.: less hardening yields better community integrity; more hardening yields worse. This directly supports the hypothesis. ✓
  • Choice B — Waterbird communities at Langford, a site with a relatively high percentage of developed land and a relatively low percentage of hardened shoreline, had lower average IWCI scores than did waterbird communities at Mill, a site with a lower percentage of developed land but a higher percentage of hardened shoreline.: the opposite pattern — hardening sites score higher, which contradicts the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice C — Waterbird communities at Stony and Curtis, two sites with relatively high percentages of developed land and hardened shoreline, had similar IWCI scores, whereas waterbird communities at Ware and Honga, two sites with relatively low percentages of developed land and hardened shoreline, had widely differing IWCI scores.: both variables move together; it is impossible to isolate hardening's effect. ✗
  • Choice D — Waterbird communities at Old Road, a site with a relatively high percentage of developed land and hardened shoreline, had lower average IWCI scores than did waterbird communities at Onancock, a site with a relatively low percentage of developed land and hardened shoreline.: again, both variables differ at once — could be development, hardening, or both. ✗
That is the correct answer.
